coredata.dc:title: Tuning the interfacial charge transfer dynamics of cellulose–graphene quantum dot nanocomposites by edge functionalization
coredata.dc:creator: Cui, Peng
Xue, Yuan
coredata.dc:description: Cellulose-graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanocomposites are emergent new materials for flexible electronics. The quantum yield of photogenerated charge carriers depends on the electronic structure and dynamics of charge transfer at the interface of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites, which is unknown. In the present work, we investigate the properties of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites in terms of functionalization of GQD based on density functional theory calculations with a conductor-like polarizable continuum model (CPCM). The cellulose–GQD nanocomposite has better photoelectric performance than GQD alone. A nonpolar solvent improves the charge transportation of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites. At the high edge coverage, functionalization of GQD with groups containing a CO double bond decreases the absorption intensity and shifts the absorption maxima of the cellulose–GQD nanocomposites to longer wavelengths. On the other hand, functional groups that do not contain a CO double bond have no effect on the absorption spectra of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites at the low edge coverage. Increasing the edge coverage of the NO2 and CHO groups stabilizes the highest occupied orbitals of the GQDs, leading to extraction of holes from the GQDs. Due to the increased solvent reorganization energy, structural flexibility, electronic coupling, and electron–phonon coupling, cellulose–GQD nanocomposites functionalized with either NO2 or CHO exhibit faster hole transfer compared to non-radiative electron-hole recombination.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fermi’s golden rule
Graphene quantum dot
Density functional theory
Chemical functionalization

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table e-component body mmlmath affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Interfacial charge separation dynamics in cellulose–GQD nanocomposite

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic representation of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites. The GQD is attached to the cellulose chain via hydrogen bonds. The GQD is functionalized by various functional groups (denoted as X) attached to its edge.

Optimized structures of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites with different functional groups (i.e. NO2, NH2, CHO, COCH3, COOH, OH, and CH3) at the GQD edges. The functional group is indicated by a dashed circle.

Charge transfer pathways (i.e., intramolecular non-radiative recombination from the HOMO to the LUMO of GQD, denoted by re; intermolecular non-radiative electron-hole recombination from the LUMO of GQD to the valence band minimum (VBM) of cellulose, denoted by re'; hole transfer from the HOMO of GQD to the VBM of cellulose, denoted by ht) between GQD and cellulose.
The HOMO and LUMO energy levels of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites functionalized with different electron-withdrawing and -donating groups. The calculation is performed in toluene.
Amount of charges transferred from the GQD moiety to the cellulose and functional group. The calculation is performed in toluene.

Projected density of states (PDOS) of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites attached with different functional groups. The dashed line denotes the Fermi level. The black, red, blue, and green lines represent the electronic states associated with the cellulose–GQD nanocomposite, cellulose, functional group, and GQD. The calculation is performed in toluene.
Absorption spectra of cellulose–GQD nanocomposite as a function of different functional groups attached to the GQD edges. The calculation is performed in toluene.
Huang–Rhys factors of GQD-7CHO@cellulose and GQD-5NO2@cellulose nanocomposites. The calculation is performed in toluene.
The calculated values of δ, TDM, γ, β, ϑ , ζ and λ of GQDs and cellulose–GQD nanocomposites. The calculation is performed in toluene.
The calculated hole transfer (ht) rates and non-radiative electron–hole recombination (re) rates in GQD-7CHO@cellulose and GQD-5NO2@cellulose nanocomposites. The calculation is performed in toluene.

head.ce:title: Tuning the interfacial charge transfer dynamics of cellulose–graphene quantum dot nanocomposites by edge functionalization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of New Materials and Shoes & Clothing Engineering, Liming Vocational University, Quanzhou 362000, Fujian Province, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of New Materials and Shoes & Clothing Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of New Materials and Shoes & Clothing Engineering, Liming Vocational University, Quanzhou 362000, Fujian Province, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, Jiangsu Province, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Nanotechnology Research Laboratory, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, Jiangsu Province, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Cellulose-graphene quantum dot (GQD) nanocomposites are emergent new materials for flexible electronics. The quantum yield of photogenerated charge carriers depends on the electronic structure and dynamics of charge transfer at the interface of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites, which is unknown. In the present work, we investigate the properties of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites in terms of functionalization of GQD based on density functional theory calculations with a conductor-like polarizable continuum model (CPCM). The cellulose–GQD nanocomposite has better photoelectric performance than GQD alone. A nonpolar solvent improves the charge transportation of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites. At the high edge coverage, functionalization of GQD with groups containing a CO double bond decreases the absorption intensity and shifts the absorption maxima of the cellulose–GQD nanocomposites to longer wavelengths. On the other hand, functional groups that do not contain a CO double bond have no effect on the absorption spectra of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites at the low edge coverage. Increasing the edge coverage of the NO2 and CHO groups stabilizes the highest occupied orbitals of the GQDs, leading to extraction of holes from the GQDs. Due to the increased solvent reorganization energy, structural flexibility, electronic coupling, and electron–phonon coupling, cellulose–GQD nanocomposites functionalized with either NO2 or CHO exhibit faster hole transfer compared to non-radiative electron-hole recombination.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have novel properties, such as tunable electronic structures and diverse surface chemistry, offering new opportunities for developing electronic devices. GQDs with regular sizes and shapes are a prerequisite for industrial applications. However, due to the heterogeneity of GQDs, the synthesis of homogeneous GQDs is not well possible with current synthetic routes. Top-down and bottom-up methods are two important approaches for preparing GQDs [1]. In the first method, GQDs are prepared by cutting graphene-based raw materials, for example, hydrothermal [2], electrochemical [3], and solvothermal [4], while in the second method, GQDs are synthesized from basic building blocks, such as polycyclic compounds and other precursors. Compared with the bottom-up method, the top-down method has advantages, including abundant raw materials and large-scale production. However, it relies on a strong oxidizing agent to introduce the oxygen-containing groups into the GQDs, which leads to changes in the flexibility and electronic conjugation of the graphene lattice.

Graphene materials agglomerate easily. In addition, they are difficult to process into films. Combining graphene with polymers to form nanocomposites not only prevents agglomeration but also increases structural flexibility. For example, Wang et al. have prepared graphene-based nanocomposites by suspending the graphene oxide sheets in polyacrylonitrile (PAN) and polyvinylpyrrolidone (PVP) using electrospinning and achieved a high conductivity of 103 S/m [5]. Chen et al. have prepared a catalyst based on nitrogen-doped GQDs combined with bismuth oxide nanosheets for the electrochemical reduction of CO2 [6]. Lv et al. have developed a cellulose–GQD nanocomposite using an in situ cultivation method [7]. Fluorescent probes sensitive to Fe3+ were fabricated from the nanocomposites prepared in this way. Moreover, the GQDs were spun onto Ag/TiO2 nanocomposites to enhance their photoreaction due to charge transfer from the Ag nanoparticles and GQDs to the TiO2 [8]. Charge separation in GQDs is crucial for enhancing their photovoltaic performance because the rapid electron–hole recombination affects the quantum yield of charge carrier photogeneration. Deciphering this information benefits the rational development of photovoltaic devices based on GQD materials.

The interfacial charge separation in polymer–nanoparticle composite relies on the energetic alignment and the charge transfer rate [9–13]. The former depends on the alignment of the energy levels between the nanoparticle and the polymer matrix, while the latter is related to electron-hole interaction, electron–phonon coupling, and free energy change [9]. The energetic alignment at the interface between the nanoparticle and the polymer matrix determines the direction of electron and hole transfer. In particular, the energy levels of fronter molecular orbitals (FMOs) in nanoparticle can be controlled by surface functionalization [14], doping [15,16], shape [17,18], size [19,20], and binding configuration [21,22], which enables photoexcited charge transfer at the interface of nanocomposites. For example, Cui et al. [9] have studied the different binding conformations of N719 dye attached to CdSe quantum dots (QDs). They found that the linkage group plays a crucial role in tuning the energetic alignment and electron–hole interaction in QD-N719 nanocomposites. Ahmad et al. [23–25] have investigated the electron-withdrawing effect of terminal acceptor on the electronic and optical properties of conjugated system for solar cell applications. Mandal et al. [18] have reported that the electron-withdrawing groups does not change the band gap of covalently functionalization of GQDs. Sheely et al. [14] have investigated the effect of edge functionalization of GQDs on their optical properties and found that the electron-inducing effect of the functional group redshifts the optical spectra. However, these studies have mainly focused on the association of geometric structure, doping, and chemical functionalization with the energy levels of FMOs in GQDs, while a more detailed analysis of electron–hole interactions, electron–phonon coupling, and free energy change during non-radiative charge transfer has yet to be conducted to gain more insight into the dynamics of charge separation in GQDs. Deciphering such information will be useful for the rational design of GQDs for photovoltaic applications.

Because of the large exciton binding energy, the Coulomb interaction between electrons and holes must be considered when modeling the charge transfer of GQDs. This requirement makes the molecular dynamics simulation computationally intensive since the Coulomb interaction must be calculated at each time step [26,27]. In contrast, time-dependent perturbation theory can easily solve the exciton problem by computing the Coulomb interaction only for the initial and final states, thus saving computational resources [28,29]. For example, Maggio et al. have studied the recombination rates of electrons between TiO2 and oxidized dye using nonadiabatic electron transfer theory [30], and their calculated recombination rates agree well with the experimental data. Liu et al. have calculated the charge recombination and charge separation rates in the P3HT/PCBM mixture using the Marcus–Levich–Jortner (M-L-J) rate equation based on time-dependent perturbation theory [31]. The calculated rates were in reasonable agreement with the experimental values. Cui et al. have investigated the effects of center and edge N-doping on the non-radiative decay dynamics of GQDs [28,32] and found that center N-doping accelerates non-radiative decay while edge N-doping slows non-radiative electron–hole recombination. Tong et al. have studied the non-radiative electron–hole recombination dynamics of cyclometallated gold(III)-arylacetylide complexes using Fermi's golden rule [29]. Their calculated non-radiative decay rates agreed well with experimental data.

The current work investigates the optoelectronic properties and charge transfer dynamics of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites, including the energetic alignment of FMOs and the non-radiative charge transfer using density functional theory (DFT)-based calculations. A series of functional groups were attached to the GQD edges to investigate the variation of the highest occupied molecular orbital (HOMO) energy levels of the GQDs with respect to the electron-withdrawing capabilities of these groups. The charge transfer rates corresponding to the different charge transfer paths at the interface are calculated using Fermi's golden rule. Our work deciphers the role of edge functionalization in modulating the charge separation dynamics of GQDs.

The interfacial charge transfer pathways of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 3 . After the photoexcitation of GQDs, electrons are promoted from occupied to unoccupied energy levels. Subsequently, the photoexcited charge carriers relax to the valence band (VB) and conduction band (CB) edges. Three non-radiative charge transfer processes take place at the band edges, namely intramolecular and intermolecular non-radiative electron-hole recombination, denoted by re and re', respectively, and hole transfer, denoted by ht. Electron transfer from the lowest unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) of GQD to the CB edge of cellulose is thermodynamically unfavorable, as discussed below; therefore, it is not considered in this work. The intermolecular electron–hole recombination is typically a much slower process than the other two processes (re and ht). Such a slow process typically results from the large difference between the corresponding energy levels of GQD and the cellulose and their separation distance, which increases the non-radiative transition barrier and reduces the electron-hole coupling. Consequently, only re and ht are considered in this work. The non-radiative charge transfer rate is calculated using the M-J-L equations [40,41]:
(1)

DA
=

ℏ
H

4 π κ

T
∑ n
e
-
S
eff
S
eff
n
n !
exp
-
Δ ∊ + κ + n ℏ
ω
eff
2
4 κ
k B
T
where
S
eff
is the averaged coupling strength of electron–phonons for high-frequency modes (>1000 cm−1), namely, the effective Huang–Rhys factor;
ω
eff
is the effective vibrational frequency;
Δ ∊
is the free energy change that is equivalent to energy difference between the initial and final states; κ is the reorganization energy that includes the contribution from solvent and intramolecular vibration; n is the quantum number that represents the vibrational energy level. The details of calculating
Δ ∊
,
S
eff
,
ω
eff
,
H
′
, and κ can be found in Supplemental materials. In previous works, the M-J-L equations have been used to calculate the non-radiative charge transfer rates of functionalized and doped GQDs [28,32] and show good agreement with experimental data.

For a cellulose–GQD nanocomposite to be a good sensitizer, electron transport, as described by electronic conductivity (δ) and chemical hardness (γ), is important. In addition, the photoelectric performance of the light sensitizer is also important for characterizing carrier mobility and is described by the chemical potential (β) and electrophilicity ( ϑ ). A smaller value of γ means that the molecule tends to be polarized and can easily exchange charges with the environment. Therefore, a smaller value of γ indicates that the molecule is soft and can easily transfer charges. Moreover, the lower the value of the β, the better the molecule is as an electron acceptor. On the other hand, the higher the ϑ , the more suitable the molecule is as an electron donor. Therefore, a lower β and a higher ϑ are desirable for a molecule to achieve better photoelectric performance. The calculation formula of δ, γ, β and ϑ are given in the Supplementary materials.

The binding energy (ζ) between cellulose and GQD reflects the structural stability of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites and is given by the energy difference between the nanocomposite and the pristine counterparts. Previous studies on QD-dye nanocomposites have shown that the dye and the QD are linked by a σ-bond [9,42]. Different binding geometries of the dye on the QD surface alter the QD-dye interaction, resulting in different σ-bond strengths and thus different binding energies between the QD and the dye. Similarly, the intermolecular hydrogen bonding is critical for the interaction between cellulose and GQD. The substitution of the functional group leads to charge redistribution in nanocomposite. Therefore, it is very interesting to see whether the charge redistribution caused by edge functionalization has an influence on the intermolecular hydrogen bonding. We calculate the binding energies and the lengths of intermolecular hydrogen bonds for cellulose–GQD nanocomposites functionalized with different electron-withdrawing and -donating groups. The calculation formula for the binding energy can be found in the Supplementary materials.

The optoelectronic properties of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites are investigated in two steps. First, the geometries of the GQDs and the cellulose chain are optimized using the PBE functional [43] together with Grimme's DFT–D3 dispersion correlation implemented in the QUICKSTEP module of the CP2K software package [44,45]. The Goedecker, Teter, and Hutter (GTH) analytical duel space pseudopotential in conjunction with double-ζ basis sets with polarization functions [44] is applied to the atomic species in the nanocomposites. The combination of the GTH pseudopotential with the DZVP basis set has been shown to be accurate in describing the electronic structures of hybrid graphene, especially at the interface [46–49]. The optimization of the wave function is performed using the efficient orbital transform (OT) method [50]. Then, the nanocomposite structures are optimized under periodic boundary conditions along the axial direction (i.e., the X-axis) of the cellulose, followed by calculations of the electronic structure using the hybrid PBE1PBE functional [51]. In the other two perpendicular directions (i.e., Y and Z axes), a 10 Å vacuum space is added to avoid self-interactions. The auxiliary basis function MOLOPT-ADMM [52], i.e., pFIT3, was used for the PBE1PBE calculation. Moreover, the Hartree-Fock exchange term is treated in the hybrid functional calculation with a long-range correction by a truncated Coulomb operator. The density cutoff for plane waves was set to 500 Ry for all calculations. According to previous studies, this value is sufficient to describe the electronic and optical properties of graphene [46,53] and recalcitrant polysaccharides [54,55]. A supercell with 20-unit cells of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites is constructed to obtain the excitation energies. Further increasing the number of unit cells does not significantly change the excitation energy, indicating that 20-unit cells are sufficient to describe the excited state properties of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites. To obtain the excitation energies, time-dependent density functional theory (TDDFT) calculations are performed, which are implemented in the CP2K software package. The calculation is performed in the solvent toluene, which is modeled with a polarizable continuum dielectric model [56]. Toluene is a typical solvent used in experiments and theoretical modeling to record absorption spectra [57–60].

Fig. 2 shows the optimized structures of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites. The CC bond length in the GQD (i.e., without being bound to the cellulose chain) is 1.42 Å and agrees well with previous theoretical report [32]. A redistribution of charges occurs when binding GQD to the cellulose chain, resulting in a larger total dipole moment (TDM) for the nanocomposites than for GQD (Table 1 ). In particular, the GQD-NH2@cellulose has the largest TDM among all nanocomposites, indicating a larger charge redistribution than the others. The γ has the same value as the band gap. The smaller the γ, the smaller the band gap, and the more reactive the molecule. Table 1 shows that binding GQD to the cellulose chain decreases γ, resulting in a larger δ for the nanocomposite than for GQD alone. Moreover, the β decreases, and the ϑ increases after GQD is bound to the cellulose chain, indicating that the cellulose–GQD nanocomposite has better photoelectric performance than GQD. Except for the β, the orders of the calculated values of other parameters of the nanocomposites are not the same as those of the GQDs, indicating that the electronic structure of GQD is changed by dipolar interaction between GQD and cellulose.

The TDMs of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites increase in acetonitrile compared to toluene (Supplemental Table S1), indicating that polar solvents lead to further redistribution of charges in cellulose–GQD nanocomposites. Moreover, the TDMs of the GQDs are increased in polar solvents due to the polarization of the GQD surface states. For most cellulose–GQD nanocomposites, the γ decreases in polar solvents, except for GQD-NH2@cellulose and GQD-OH@cellulose. In these GQDs, the polar solvent stabilizes their surface states, thus increasing the band gaps of the nanocomposites due to the FMOs of nanocomposites are governed by GQD states (Fig. 6). The γ of the GQDs does not change with solvent polarity, except for GQD-NH2 and GQD-NO2; this indicates that functionalization with NH2 and NO2 significantly destabilizes the GQD surfaces and increases the γ of the functionalized GQDs. The dipole–dipole interaction between the GQDs and the solvent molecules changes the FMOs of GQDs [9], altering the chemical reactivity.

The β-values of GQDs decrease in polar solvent (Supplemental Table S1), while they increase in polar solvent for cellulose–GQD nanocomposites, suggesting that the formation of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites balances the solvent effect, leading to a decrease in the ability to attract electrons in polar solvents. The ϑ -values of cellulose–GQD, GQD-CH3@cellulose, and GQDs increase in polar solvent, while it either decreases or remains the same for the other cellulose–GQD nanocomposites in polar solvent. Overall, a nonpolar solvent, such as toluene, improves the charge transfer ability and, thus, the photoelectric performance of most of the cellulose–GQD nanocomposites in this study.

The changes in ζ-values of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites with respect to functional group variation are negligible, with values between −1.44 and −1.45 eV. However, the ζ of GQD-NH2@cellulose is −1.60 eV, much higher than that of the others. This higher binding energy is caused by the larger charge redistribution in GQD-NH2@cellulose, which leads to an enhanced GQD-cellulose interaction, as evidenced by the shorter length (λ) of the intermolecular hydrogen bond. Increasing the polarity of the solvent reduces the dipole moments at the GQD surface [9,42], thereby reducing the GQD-cellulose interaction as well as increasing the lengths of the intermolecular hydrogen bonds (Supplemental Table S1).

Depending on whether the functional group contains CO double bonds, the GQDs are divided into two categories: those with CO double bonds, that is, GQD-COCH3, GQD-CHO, and GQD-COOH, and those without CO double bonds, that is, GQD-CH3, GQD-OH, and GQD-NH2. A previous study [61] has shown that the CO double bond expands the conjugated π system of the carbon skeleton and lowers the HOMO and LUMO levels of the GQD. In particular, the LUMO level decreases more than the HOMO level, leading to a decrease in the band gap of GQDs functionalized with a group containing a CO double bond (i.e., COOH, COCH3, NO2, and CHO). In contrast, the HOMO and LUMO of the GQD functionalized with a functional group lacking a CO double bond (i.e., NH2, OH, and CH3) shift to higher energy levels. In particular, the HOMO level shifts more than that of the LUMO, which reduces the band gap of the GQD. In addition, another study [14] has shown that the optical properties of GQDs are significantly affected by the edge coverage of electron-withdrawing groups, inspiring this investigation of the effects of the passivation density of the functional group on the optoelectronic properties of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites.

Fig. 6 shows the projected density of states (PDOS) of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites attached with various functional groups. The GQD states dominate the FMOs of the nanocomposites, while the occupied and unoccupied cellulose states sit in the much lower and higher energy levels of the nanocomposite. The energetic alignment between the GQD and cellulose ensures that the GQD is the main light absorber and emitter and that no intermolecular charge transfer occurs between the GQD and cellulose upon photoexcitation of the GQD. Such an energetic alignment is consistent with the experimental report [7] in which cellulose–GQD nanocomposite was used as a photoluminescence probe to detect Fe3+. Otherwise, the intermolecular charge transfer between the GQD and the cellulose would quench the photoluminescence, resulting in Fe3+ not being detected. Except for the COCH3-functionalized cellulose–GQD nanocomposites, the difference between the HOMO energy levels of the cellulose and the GQD containing a CO double bond decreases with the increase of passivation density of the functional group (Supplementary Figures S5, S6, S7). The low electron-withdrawing ability of –COCH3 results in the lowest negative induction effect in the GQD. Consequently, there is a negligible energy shift in the HOMO level.

In addition, the high passivation density of CHO and NO2 (Supplementary Fig. S5(d) and S7(c) and (d)) raises the HOMO level of cellulose to a higher position than that of GQD, which creates favorable conditions for the extraction of holes from GQD. In particular, the difference between the HOMO energy levels of GQD and cellulose is reduced more with the increase of the passivation density of NO2 than with the CHO. This is due to the stronger electron-withdrawing ability of NO2, resulting in greater stabilization of the HOMO energy levels of NO2-functionalized cellulose–GQD nanocomposites. However, the energy gap remains almost the same when the number of NO2 groups increases from five to seven, indicating that the influence of edge functionalization on the GQD has peaked; therefore, further increasing the passivation density brings no significant changes in the electronic structure of the GQD.

Although the difference between the HOMO energy levels of cellulose and GQD in the COOH-functionalized cellulose–GQD nanocomposite decreases with the increase in COOH groups bound to the GQD edges, the HOMO level is still determined by the GQD state with the highest edge coverage (Supplemental Figure S6 (d)). This is because, under a high passivation density, significant structural deformation of the GQD occurs [14], leading to electron localization in the GQD. The different extent of the reduction in the energy gaps of COOH–, NO2–, and CHO-functionalized cellulose–GQD nanocomposites is due to the combined effect of structural deformation and negative induction of the electron-withdrawing group, leading to different degrees of stabilization of the HOMO energy levels of the GQDs.

Increasing the GQD sizes has a small effect on the energy alignment of the GQDs and the cellulose in the occupied levels, because the effective mass of the holes is larger than that of the electrons in the GQDs [62]. Therefore, the HOMO energy level of GQDs is insignificantly affected by the size of the GQDs. Therefore, our conclusion about the thermodynamic conditions for the extraction of holes from GQDs is valid even if the size of the GQDs increases.

The first absorption peak of the non-functionalized cellulose–GQD nanocomposite is at about 1 eV, while the main absorption peak is about 2.5 eV (Fig. 7 (a)). The first and main absorption peaks of the non-functionalized cellulose–GQD nanocomposite are red-shifted compared to the GQD (i.e., 3.1 eV and 4.5 eV [61]) because the formation of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites leads to a decrease in the band gap, as described above. The NH2 functionalization redshifts the first absorption peak, while the OH functionalization blueshifts the first absorption peak. In addition, functionalization with CHO and NO2 redshifts the main absorption peak. Except for the CH3-functionalized cellulose–GQD nanocomposite, the absorption intensities of the other functionalized nanocomposites are lower than that of the non-functionalized nanocomposite. Increasing the edge coverage of functional groups containing a CO double bond decreases the absorption intensities and redshifts the absorption maxima of the functionalized cellulose–GQD nanocomposites at a high passivation density (Supplemental Figures S9 (c), S10 (c), S11 (c), and S13 (c)). In contrast, no significant effect on the absorption spectra is observed for the cellulose–GQD nanocomposites with low edge coverage by functional groups that do not contain a CO double bond (Supplemental Figures S9 (a) and (b), Figures S10 (a) and (b), Figures S11 (a) and (b), and Figures S13 (a) and (b)). The polar solvent increases the intensities of the absorption maxima of the functionalized cellulose–GQD nanocomposites for high edge coverage by functional groups containing a CO double bond (Supplemental Figures S15-21).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interfacial charge separation dynamics in cellulose–GQD nanocomposite
ce:section.ce:para: As mentioned earlier, two charge transfer pathways, ht and re (Fig. 3), are two competitive processes in cellulose–GQD nanocomposites. In the first case, the larger effective mass of holes [62] is the dominant factor slowing down the charge transfer process, while in the second case, a large potential energy barrier because of the large energy gap has to be overcome for the non-radiative transition. Therefore, we investigate the two charge transfer processes in cellulose–GQD nanocomposites functionalized with five NO2 groups or seven CHO groups since the binding of five NO2 groups and seven CHO groups to the edges of the GQD provides favorable thermodynamic conditions for the hole injection from the GQD into the cellulose, as described above.

The ht times range from 0.1 to 2 ns (Table 2 ), in good agreement with the nanosecond timescale that has been reported in previous experimental [59] and theoretical [63] studies, while the times for re range from 0.01 to 0.2 ps, in good agreement with the sub-picosecond time scale reported in a recent experiment [64]. The faster ht rates than the re rates indicate efficient charge separation due to the electronic interface structure. In particular, the ht rate of GQD-7CHO@cellulose is faster than that of GQD-5NO2@cellulose, while the re rate of GQD-7CHO@cellulose is slower than that of GQD-5NO2@cellulose, suggesting that CHO substitution leads to more efficient charge separation in the cellulose–GQD nanocomposite than NO2 substitution.

Equation (1) shows that the non-radiative charge transfer rate is determined by the free energy change, the effective Huang-Rhys factor, the intramolecular and solvent reorganization energies, and the electronic coupling. The charge transfer rate increases with the decrease of the free energy change and increases with the increase of the reorganization energy as they appear in the nominator of the last exponential term of equation (1). Therefore, a smaller free energy change and a larger intramolecular and solvent reorganization energy reduce the potential energy barrier of the non-radiative transition and increase the non-radiative charge transfer rate, i.e., the energy gap law [65]. The free energy change (approximated by the emission energy of the GQDs) for the re is determined by the quantum confinement of the GQDs, while the difference between the HOMO energy levels of the GQDs and the cellulose determines the ht. The emission energy of GQD-7CHO is larger than that of GQD-5NO2. This indicates that the substitution of CHO at the edges of the GQD leads to larger quantum confinement in the GQDs than the substitution of NO2 at the edges of the GQD.

According to equation (1), the non-radiative charge transfer rate is proportional to the electronic coupling. The electronic coupling of the re is determined by the Coulomb interaction between the electrons and the holes, that is, the exciton effect. In contrast, the electronic coupling of the ht is determined by the HOMO-HOMO interaction between the GQD and the cellulose chain [9]. The larger electronic coupling of the ht than that of the re indicates a stronger electronic interaction between HOMO orbitals than between the LUMO and HOMO orbitals for both GQD-7CHO@cellulose and GQD-5NO2@cellulose. The electronic coupling of ht is larger for GQD-7CHO@cellulose than for GQD-5NO2@cellulose, while the electronic coupling of re is larger for GQD-5NO2@cellulose than for GQD-7CHO@cellulose. This indicates that the HOMO-HOMO interaction between the GQD and cellulose is larger in GQD-7CHO@cellulose than in GQD-5NO2@cellulose, while the HOMO-LUMO interaction of the GQD is larger in GQD-5NO2@cellulose than in GQD-7CHO@cellulose. The latter indicates that the exciton confinement is lower in a GQD functionalized with seven CHO groups than in a GQD functionalized with five NO2 groups, resulting in a stronger electron–hole interaction in GQD-5NO2. The CO double bond extends the π-system of the GQD moiety, reducing the confinement of the charge carriers. As more functional groups with CO double bonds are added to the edges of the GQD, the confinement of the excitons decreases.

The Huang–Rhys factor describes the strength of electron–phonon coupling during a non-radiative transition [28,32]. During the non-radiative charge transfer, the released electronic energy is converted into phonon energies having multiple vibrational modes. In particular, the influence of the low-frequency vibrational modes on the non-radiative charge transfer is described by the intramolecular reorganization energy, while for high-frequency vibrational modes, it is described by the effective Huang–Rhys factor. A larger intramolecular reorganization energy indicates that the molecular structure is more flexible, and thus, non-radiative charge transfer can be facilitated by slow acoustic modes. A larger effective Huang–Rhys factor suggests that the high-frequency optical stretching and bending modes contribute more to accelerating non-radiative charge relaxation. The Huang–Rhys factors of GQD-7CHO@cellulose are larger than those of GQD-5NO2@cellulose because the –7CHO has less mass than the –5NO2, resulting in a lower mass for the GQD-7CHO@cellulose. Therefore, the non-radiative charge transfer in GQD-7CHO@cellulose is coupled with more high-frequency vibrational modes with higher strength.

For GQD-7CHO@cellulose, the ht leads to a larger intramolecular reorganization energy than re, while for GQD-5NO2@cellulose, the change in intramolecular reorganization energies for ht and re is negligible. The intramolecular reorganization energies for both re and ht are larger in GQD-7CHO@cellulose than GQD-5NO2@cellulose, indicating that the structure of GQD-7CHO@cellulose is more flexible than that of GQD-5NO2@cellulose. The effective Huang–Rhys factors of GQD-7CHO@cellulose are larger than those of GQD-5NO2@cellulose because the electronic transition in GQD-7CHO@cellulose couples with more nuclear vibration modes and therefore induces more relaxation channels (Fig. 8 ). In contrast, the relaxation channels in GQD-5NO2@cellulose are limited for both ht and re, resulting in smaller effective Huang–Rhys factors. Moreover, the Huang–Rhys factors for ht are larger than those for re, suggesting that the high-frequency vibrational modes favor ultrafast hole transfer in GQD-7CHO@cellulose and GQD-5NO2@cellulose.

The solvent reorganization energy of the re is smaller than that of the ht due to the larger variation of the dipole moments arise from the hole transfer than that from the vertical excitation in the GQDs. This is because hole transfer leads to a substantial charge redistribution between the GQD and the cellulose, resulting in a larger difference in the dipole moments between the donor and acceptor states. Correspondingly, the lowest excitation causes only charge redistribution within GQD, resulting in a smaller difference in the dipole moments between the donor and acceptor states. The reorganization energy of the solvent is proportional to the square of the difference in the dipole moments between the donor and acceptor states. Therefore, the solvent reorganization energies for ht are larger than those for re. Moreover, the solvent reorganization energy for ht is smaller for GQD-7CHO@cellulose than for GQD-5NO2@cellulose, while the opposite is true for the solvent reorganization energy for re.

Overall, the higher value of the ht rate compared to the re rate is due to the larger reorganization energy of the solvent, the effective Huang-Rhys factor, and the electronic coupling. The faster ht rate in GQD-7CHO@cellulose than in GQD-5NO2@cellulose is due to the larger intramolecular reorganization energy, larger effective Huang-Rhys factor and larger electronic coupling. This suggests that the solvent effect, optical phonon modes, structural flexibility, and exciton effect, rather than the energy gap law, are the dominant factors leading to faster ht rate than to re rate. In contrast, the faster re rate in GQD-5NO2@cellulose compared to GQD-7CHO@cellulose is caused by a smaller free energy change and stronger electronic coupling, indicating that both the energy gap law and the exciton effect are the dominant mechanism leading to faster re rate in GQD-5NO2@cellulose.

This work investigates the optoelectronic properties of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites with chemical functional groups attached to the GQD edges with different electron-donating and electron-withdrawing capabilities. The formation of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites leads to a change in electron transfer performance due to charge redistribution between the GQD and cellulose chain. The functional groups containing a CO double bond extend the conjugated π-system of the carbon skeleton of the GQDs, leading to a downward shift of the energy levels of the FMOs. In combination with the passivation density, this has a significant impact on the optical properties of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites. In particular, increasing the edge coverage of functional groups containing a CO double bond reduces the absorption intensity and redshifts the absorption maxima of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites under the high edge coverage.

A nonpolar solvent decreases the chemical potentials and increases the electrophilicity of cellulose–GQD nanocomposites, benefitting photoelectric performance. Increasing the polarity of the solvent increases the amplitude of the main absorption peak of cellulose–GQD nanocomposite with a high passivation density of functional groups that do not contain a CO double bond.

The hole transfer rates are faster than the non-radiative electron–hole recombination rates for cellulose–GQD nanocomposites functionalized by NO2 and CHO groups at high edge coverages, indicating efficient interfacial charge separation between the GQD and the cellulose chain in these nanocomposites. In particular, the substitution of CHO groups at the edges of GQD leads to a more efficient charge separation than the substitution of NO2. Such efficient charge separation is related to the solvent effect, optical phonon modes of nucleus, structural flexibility and exciton effect.
Peng Cui: Conceptualization, Software, Methodology, Validation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Yuan Xue: Funding acquisition, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.flatc.2022.100454.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mueller ML, Yan X, McGuire JA, Li L-s. Triplet states and electronic relaxation in photoexcited graphene quantum dots. Nano letters. 2010;10(7):2679-82.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electronic and optical properties of edge-functionalized graphene quantum dots and the underlying mechanism
J. Phys. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Li Y, Shu H, Niu X, Wang J. Electronic and optical properties of edge-functionalized graphene quantum dots and the underlying mechanism. The Journal of Physical Chemistry C. 2015;119(44):24950-7.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effective mass of electrons and holes in bilayer graphene: Electron-hole asymmetry and electron-electron interaction
Phys. Rev. B

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zou K, Hong X, Zhu J. Effective mass of electrons and holes in bilayer graphene: Electron-hole asymmetry and electron-electron interaction. Physical Review B. 2011;84(8):085408.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Manipulating the phonon bottleneck in graphene quantum dots: phonon-induced carrier relaxation within the linear response theory
J. Phys. Chem. C

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Trinastic JP, Chu I-H, Cheng H-P. Manipulating the phonon bottleneck in graphene quantum dots: phonon-induced carrier relaxation within the linear response theory. The Journal of Physical Chemistry C. 2015;119(39):22357-69.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Subpicosecond Hot Hole Transfer in a Graphene Quantum Dot Composite with High Efficiency
J. Phys. Chem. Lett.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mishra K, Acharjee D, Das A, Ghosh S. Subpicosecond Hot Hole Transfer in a Graphene Quantum Dot Composite with High Efficiency. The Journal of Physical Chemistry Letters. 2022;13(2):606-13.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The Golden Rule. Application for fun and profit in electron transfer, energy transfer, and excited-state decay
PCCP

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Ito A, Meyer TJ. The Golden Rule. Application for fun and profit in electron transfer, energy transfer, and excited-state decay. Physical Chemistry Chemical Physics. 2012;14(40):13731-45.
